feedback.text: Overall, this is a very pro-consumer suggestion, and I fully support it. For far too long, publishers and shady developers have been able to take advantage of young people with addictive loot boxes, have been able to farm our data for sale, have made free trials a complete gamble whether or not we can cancel it and have used media and influencers as shady tools for marketing. However, I would like to add a 6th point to the Fairness Act, as it relates to Video Games. Right now, publishers of Video Games have the power to completely remove access to their products that the consumer has paid for, by building a product that only works online, or requires online access to play, publishers may effectively destroy the game we have already paid for. I feel this is Unfair in the Digital Market, and I would love if the EU would rectify it with a law to prevent effectively scamming customers. For an example, imagine someone bought a book. They can read it in their living room with no issue, but every time they open the cover, they need to authenticate the purchase with the publisher, to make sure the copy is authentic. One day, the servers cost too much to maintain, and the publisher quietly, but permanently, shuts them down, offering no alternatives for the consumer who paid for his book to access it. Furthermore, the EULA dictates that the book must be destroyed and the publisher sets the copy on fire. This is essentially what is happening in the Video Game industry right now. I implore the law makers to stop this unfair practice in addition to all the other good issues already depicted in the Act.
